author: claude
content:
needs-human — ADR-0001 reframed on #1154 (feat/stealth-js-instrument-v2). Pushed; PR MERGEABLE. Existential keep-vs-remove call below for Stefan.

## (a) What was cut / reframed

Cut the entire development-era narration — the "the first draft anticipated a capture-XOR-native frontier / building the PR collapsed that frontier / the draft missed / originally listed alongside" arc that ran through Context, Prior work, Residual gaps, Tamper-resilience, Decision, Consequences and Precursors. That was assumptions-held-while-refining-the-setup, not the decision. Facts that survived the collapse (arity / `toString` / `window.name` captured natively) are now stated as plain facts, not as surprises that overturned a prior fear.

Reframed to the actual conclusion up front: stealth subsumes legacy on both primary axes (detectability and tamper-resilience); legacy is detectable by design and strictly worse on both. The "Prior work" section (a long literature walk that mostly existed to justify the abandoned framing) was dropped; the one durable citation (Krumnow et al. 2022, the threat model) is folded into Context. "Residual gaps" as a standalone section is gone — its two real items are now the spine of the Decision.

File renamed `0001-stealth-legacy-coexistence.md` → `0001-retain-legacy-js-instrument.md` and both references updated (`docs/index.rst`, `docs/developers/Stealth-Instrumentation.md`). "Coexistence" implied a live, symmetric tension that no longer exists.

## (b) Recommendation: KEEP, reframed (not remove)

A genuine decision remains, and it is exactly the one your feedback predicted: **why retain legacy at all rather than delete it, once stealth subsumes it.** That is a real, contestable engineering call with a cost on both sides:

- Delete: permanently lose two narrow but genuine capture modes with no stealth equivalent (recursion into plain `Object`/`Array` containers; per-instance attribution of shared-prototype methods).
- Retain behind mutual-exclusion: pay maintenance on dead-for-most-users code, at zero runtime cost to the stealth default.

We chose retention until those residuals are closed in stealth or shown unused. That is a decision worth recording — a future maintainer who sees stealth dominating on every headline axis will reasonably ask "why is this old detectable instrument still here?", and this ADR answers it. So I did NOT gut it to nothing; the reframe has a spine. Recommend keep.

The one thing I could NOT decide for you and explicitly left to you: whether, even with a real decision present, you'd still rather this live as a code comment / CHANGELOG note than a standing ADR. I judged it ADR-worthy (it's an architectural retention decision with a live follow-up, #1187, gating the future overlap-validation model), but the "should an ADR exist at all" bar is yours.

## (c) New framing / title

- **Title:** "Retain the legacy JS instrument as a narrow capture fallback to stealth"
- **Spine:** Context establishes stealth-subsumes-legacy as settled fact (not a journey) → Decision is the retention call and its why-not-delete reasoning → Tamper-resilience and the #1187 open question (overlap-validation viability) support it.
- **Status:** Accepted, dated "reframed 2026-06-19".

## Before / after — opening

BEFORE (Context, first two paras):
> The stealth instrument intercepts APIs with `Proxy`/`exportFunction` across Firefox's **Xray boundary**. The first draft of this ADR anticipated a broad *capture-XOR-native frontier*: it claimed that boundary fundamentally prevents a privileged-side wrapper from impersonating a *native* accessor for a whole class of cheaply-fingerprinted built-ins … The thesis was that for any such API, capture and undetectability are mutually exclusive … and therefore stealth *cannot* subsume legacy.
> **Building the PR largely collapsed that frontier.** All three named obstacles turned out to be capturable *and* undetectable at the same time …

AFTER (Context opening):
> Stealth subsumes legacy on every primary axis: [detectability — every paper-named artifact eliminated; tamper-resilience — both threats require prior detection, which stealth denies]. Legacy is therefore detectable by design and, through that detectability, exposed to tampering. It is strictly worse than stealth on both axes. This raises the decision this ADR records: **once stealth subsumes legacy, why keep legacy at all rather than delete it?**
